coredata.dc:title: Key factors determining competitions between double-stranded RNAs in Tribolium castaneum
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Xu
Peng, Yingchuan
Song, Huifang
Wang, Jinda
Zhao, Jun
Tang, Peian
Han, Zhaojun
Wang, Kangxu
coredata.dc:description: Combinatorial delivery of different double-stranded RNAs (dsRNAs) can result in competitive inhibition in insect pests and remains one of the obstacles in the way of future applications of the RNA interference (RNAi)-based pest control. In this study, we attempted to discover the basic competition characteristics between dsRNAs and provided insight into the solutions of competitive inhibition. RNAi sensitive insect species Tribolium castaneum were treated, and competitions between dsRNA fragments influencing the effectiveness of RNAi response could be measured. A chimeric dsRNA strategy for conjugating different dsRNA fragments into a single molecule and a nanoparticle carbon quantum dots-mediated dsRNA delivery were confirmed as efficient methods to knock down multiple target genes simultaneously. Furthermore, in vitro assays were conducted for determining the accumulation speed of serially diluted and incubated dsRNA in the midgut tissues. Our data showed that the accumulation of dsRNAs of different treated amounts was 0.25 μg ≈ 0.5 μg > 1 μg ≥ 2 μg > 4 μg, indicating that accumulation speed would be affected by treated dsRNA. Overall, our results strongly suggest that endocytic components influencing cellular uptake might be oversaturated when an excess amount of dsRNAs were treated, thereby causing competitive inhibition of target genes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: RNAi
dsRNAs
Competitive inhibition
Tribolium castaneum
Pest control

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: RNA isolation, cDNA synthesis, and RT-qPCR
In vitro midgut dsRNA accumulation assays
Competitive inhibition occurred between the different dsRNA molecules
Competitive inhibition could be decreased by conjugated chimeric dsRNAs
Competitive inhibition could be decreased by CQD encapsulated dsRNAs
Accumulation of a series of treated dsRNA amounts in T. castaneum midgut

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key R&D Program of China ( 2019YFC1605303 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 32102296 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BK20210669 ), the National Grain Industry Foundation for Young Top-Notch Scholars of China ( LQ2020102 ), the Youth Scholar Support Program of Nanjing University of Finance and Economics (NUFE), the Science-Technology Foundation for Outstanding Young Scientists of Henan Academy of Agricultural Sciences ( 2020YQ21 ), the Faculty Startup Program of NUFE, and the Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD) .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Relative expressions of TcTUB mRNA after (A) injection and (B) feeding of the indicated dsRNAs, dsT: dsTcTUB and dsE: dsEYFP. dsE was used as control. Each treatment contained at least five replicates, with three to five individuals included in each replicate. The relative expression was calculated based on the control. Different letters on the treatment group indicate significant differences at p < 0.05 (Tukey's test).

Gene-silencing effects after treatment with corresponding 250 bp of gene-specific dsRNA fragments, a mixture of two dsRNAs, and the chimeric dsRNA (dsC). (A) The chimeric dsRNA comprising 250 bp of dsEYFP (dsE) and dsTcTUB (dsT) were used. Relative expressions of TcTUB mRNA after (B) injection and (C) feeding with the indicated dsRNAs. dsE was used as control. Each treatment contained at least 10 replicates, with three to five individuals included in a replicate. The relative expression was calculated based on the control. Different letters on the treatment group indicate significant differences at p < 0.05 (Tukey's test).

Gene-silencing effects in the T. castaneum Pu11 individuals after treatment with corresponding 250 bp of gene-specific dsRNA fragments, a mixture of two dsRNAs, and the chimeric dsRNA (dsC). (A) Relative expressions of TcTUB mRNA after injecting with the indicated dsRNAs. (B) Relative expressions of EYFP mRNA after injecting with the indicated dsRNAs. (C) Relative expressions of TcTUB mRNA after feeding with the indicated dsRNAs. (D) Relative expressions of EYFP mRNA after feeding with the indicated dsRNAs. The 250 bp double-stranded red fluorescent protein (dsRED) fragment was used as control. Each treatment contained at least five replicates, with three to five individuals included in a replicate. The relative expression was calculated based on the control. Different letters on the treatment group indicate significant differences at p < 0.05 (Tukey's test). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The nanoparticle CQD-mediated gene-silencing effects in the T. castaneum Pu11 individuals after injecting individuals with corresponding 250 bp of gene-specific dsRNA fragments, a mixture of two dsRNAs, and the chimeric dsRNA (dsC). (A) Relative expressions of TcTUB mRNA after injecting the CQD-encapsulated indicated dsRNAs. (B) Relative expressions of EYFP mRNA after injecting the CQD-encapsulated indicated dsRNAs. (C) Relative expressions of TcTUB mRNA after feeding with the indicated dsRNAs. (D) Relative expressions of EYFP mRNA after feeding with the indicated dsRNAs. The 250 bp double-stranded red fluorescent protein (dsRED) fragment was used as control. Each treatment contained at least five replicates, with three to five individuals included in each replicate. The relative expression was calculated based on the control. Different letters on the treatment group indicate significant differences at p < 0.05 (Tukey's test). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Cellular uptake ability associated with treated double-stranded RNA amount. A series of dsRED amounts were incubated (in vitro) with T. castaneum midgut samples over the experimental period. Different letters on the treatment group indicate significant differences at p < 0.05 (Tukey's test).
Information of the double-stranded RNA used.

row.entry: targeting the Pu11 individuals' EYFP; used as control in GA-1 individuals
used as control in Pu11 individuals
targeting TcTub and EYFP in Pu11 individuals

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ds: double-stranded; Tc: T. castaneum; TUB: α-tubulin; EYFP: enhanced yellow fluorescent protein; RED: red fluorescent protein; C: chimeric dsRNA; T7: TAATACGACTCACTATAGGG.
Tc: T. castaneum; RP49: ribosomal protein 49; RPS3: ribosomal protein S3.

head.ce:title: Key factors determining competitions between double-stranded RNAs in Tribolium castaneum

Nanjing University of Finance and Economics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Combinatorial delivery of different double-stranded RNAs (dsRNAs) can result in competitive inhibition in insect pests and remains one of the obstacles in the way of future applications of the RNA interference (RNAi)-based pest control. In this study, we attempted to discover the basic competition characteristics between dsRNAs and provided insight into the solutions of competitive inhibition. RNAi sensitive insect species Tribolium castaneum were treated, and competitions between dsRNA fragments influencing the effectiveness of RNAi response could be measured. A chimeric dsRNA strategy for conjugating different dsRNA fragments into a single molecule and a nanoparticle carbon quantum dots-mediated dsRNA delivery were confirmed as efficient methods to knock down multiple target genes simultaneously. Furthermore, in vitro assays were conducted for determining the accumulation speed of serially diluted and incubated dsRNA in the midgut tissues. Our data showed that the accumulation of dsRNAs of different treated amounts was 0.25 μg ≈ 0.5 μg > 1 μg ≥ 2 μg > 4 μg, indicating that accumulation speed would be affected by treated dsRNA. Overall, our results strongly suggest that endocytic components influencing cellular uptake might be oversaturated when an excess amount of dsRNAs were treated, thereby causing competitive inhibition of target genes.

ce:section.ce:para: RNA interference (RNAi) is an evolutionarily conserved mechanism where a double-stranded RNA (dsRNA) inhibits target gene expression by triggering sequence-specific mRNA destruction (Iwasaki et al., 2015). In agricultural science, RNAi has been widely used for gene identification. It also provides a promising tool for managing pests using a gene-specific dsRNA-based insecticide (Kim et al., 2015; Lü et al., 2021; Pan et al., 2020). However, several studies found that combinatorial delivery of different dsRNAs can result in competitive inhibition in insect pests and remains one of the obstacles in the way of future applications of the RNAi-based pest control (Miller et al., 2012; Miyata et al., 2014; Tomoyasu et al., 2008).

Excess amounts of exogenously introduced dsRNA or siRNA can saturate the cellular RNAi machinery in vivo and in vitro (Loinger et al., 2012). One consequence of such saturation is competition between dsRNAs over a limited pool of shared RNAi machinery components and/or cell entry and transport components required for processing and biogenesis (Koller et al., 2006). This competitive inhibition usually decreases the efficacy of the dsRNAs involved in the competition, evident by an inability to downregulate target gene(s) (Vickers et al., 2007). Although the dsRNA competition is well elucidated in higher mammals (Loinger et al., 2012), to the best of our knowledge, they have not been systematically analyzed using different insect models, which could be particularly important to future RNAi-based pest management. For example, occasionally, applications require silencing multiple genes in particular species, but combinatorial delivery of different dsRNAs can initiate competitive inhibition among these genes (Miller et al., 2012). Moreover, usually, more than one pest species exists in real circumstances; hence a formulation with different dsRNAs could trigger RNAi on multiple insect species, and differentially treated dsRNAs could compete with each other as well (Wang et al., 2021).

The red flour beetle (Tribolium castaneum) is one of the major destructive stored product pests. Since T. castaneum has developed high resistance levels to some major pesticides, the traditional management methods are gradually losing their effectiveness. However, many studies indicated that RNAi in T. castaneum is easy to achieve by dsRNA injection and feeding, making this insect a very promising model to study the mechanisms and applications of RNAi (Campbell et al., 2021). Therefore, this research dissected the competition between dsRNAs and deciphered the inner mechanisms underlying competitions in T. castaneum, thereby establishing an efficient and convenient method to decrease the competitive effects in insects. The study of these basic competitive structures between dsRNAs in T. castaneum will provide insight into the molecular mechanism of the competitive inhibition and aid in RNAi-based pest control.

The Georgia-1 (GA-1) T. castaneum (Coleoptera, Tenebrionidae) strain was reared in a growth chamber with controlled temperature (30 ± 1 °C), dark conditions, and humidity 62–65% and fed on whole wheat flour containing 5% yeast. The nub enhancer trap line Pu11, which has enhanced yellow fluorescent protein (EYFP) expression, used in chimeric dsRNA assays, was reared under the same conditions as the GA-1 strain (Lai et al., 2018).

dsRNAs were generated via in vitro transcription using a T7 RiboMAX system (Promega, Madison, WI, USA), according to the manufacturer's protocol. A DNA template flanked by two T7 promoter sequences was synthesized to produce dsRNA. The primers used in dsRNA synthesis are listed in Table 1 . The concentration of each transcript was determined using a NanoDrop ND-2000 spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA).

T. castaneum larvae were injected for dsRNA injections as described previously (Linz et al., 2014). For each experimental condition, 25–35 of the last instar larvae were injected from one treatment. Approximately 0.5 μL of dsRNA solution was injected into the hemocoel at the dorsal side of the first abdominal segment (A1) using a Nanoliter 2010 injector with a MICRO4 Controller (World Precision Instruments, Sarasota, FL, USA). The insects administered dsRNA via injection were reared routinely for 72 h before RNAi effect analysis.

For dsRNA ingestions, T. castaneum larvae were fed whole wheat flour mixed with the dsRNA (Peng et al., 2020). The mixture was formed by adding 50 μL of dsRNA solutions to 0.5 g of the flour. After completely air-drying, the mixture was further mashed into a powder. Then, the mixed feed powder was dispensed into a 96-well plate at a cell density of approximately 5 mg, containing 0.5–2.5 μg dsRNA, per well per larvae. After 72 h, feeding groups were examined for the RNAi effects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RNA isolation, cDNA synthesis, and RT-qPCR
ce:section.ce:para: RNAi effects were evaluated with Real Time-quantitative PCR (RT-qPCR) by examining target gene expressions. Insect samples were collected and kept in liquid nitrogen. For each treatment, total RNA was isolated from three to five individuals with an RNeasy Mini Kit (Qiagen, Valencia, CA, USA), according to the manufacturer's protocol. The quality and quantity of RNA was assessed using a 1.5% gel electrophoresis and NanoDrop ND-2000 spectrophotometer. cDNA was reverse-transcribed from 1 μg of total RNA with HiScript III-RT SuperMix for RT-qPCR (+gDNA wiper) (Vazyme, Nanjing, JS, China). RT-qPCR reactions were performed using an AceQ qPCR SYBR Green Master Mix (Vazyme) and an ABI 7500 Real-Time PCR system (Applied Biosystems, Foster City, CA, USA). The PCR conditions were 95 °C for 30 s, followed by 40 cycles at 95 °C for 5 s, and 60 °C for 34 s. Ribosomal protein 49 (TcRP49) and ribosomal protein S3 (TcRPS3) of the housekeeping genes were used as internal references for T. castaneum reactions. For each gene, at least five biological replicates with three technical replicates were tested. The relative expression level was determined by the geometric mean of the expression level calculated by the 2−ΔΔCt method with each separate reference gene using the Applied Biosystems 7500 software (version 2.0.4). Primer pairs were validated by standard curve analysis, and primers are presented in Table 2 .

As shown in Fig. 3A, the 250-bp dsTcTUB and dsEYFP fragments were concatemerized into a 500-bp chimeric dsRNA (dsC). The templates of the chimeric dsRNA were synthesized by GenScript (Nanjing, China) with a T7 sequence. The chimeric dsRNAs transcripts were generated in vitro using the T7 RiboMAX system, and dsRNA treatment was via intraabdominal injections. RNA isolation, reverse transcription, and RT-qPCR evaluation can aid assess the gene-silencing effects.

Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) were synthesized utilizing a microwave strategy (Das et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2019). Polyethylene glycol (PEG-200, Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA; 9 mL) was added to 3 mL of RNase-free water. Polyethylenimine (PEI; 100 mg; Sigma-Aldrich) in 2 mL RNase-free water was mixed with the PEG-200 solution, and the mixture was microwaved at 800 W for 3 min. A CQD-dsRNA mix (ratio, 20:1) was suspended in a sodium sulfate solution at 4 °C and incubated overnight. The CQD-dsRNA mixture was centrifuged at 21,000 xg for 30 min to measure the encapsulation efficacies of CQD, and the concentration of dsRNA in the supernatant was measured using a spectrophotometer. The loading efficacy between the starting dsRNA amounts and the remaining amounts in the supernatants was calculated to be approximately 95%. Before the cotreatment assays, competitor and reporter dsRNAs were mixed and diluted to a final concentration of 1 μg/μL. After that, insect samples (Pu11) were injected into the diluted mixture. Following RNA extraction, reverse transcription and CQD-mediated RNAi effects were determined by RT-qPCR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro midgut dsRNA accumulation assays
ce:section.ce:para: Whole gut tissues were dissected from the last instar larvae of GA-1 T. castaneum, and tissue samples were placed in saline. dsRED (the dsRNA of red fluorescent protein) solutions were diluted using the EX-CELL® 405 Serum-Free Medium for Insect Cells (Sigma-Aldrich) according to a previously described method (Wang et al., 2018). Isolated gut tissues were rinsed three times with the medium before being extended on wax, and the solution was then pipetted (25 °C; relative humidity: 90–95%) until the entire midgut area was covered. Gut samples were cultured for 5, 30, and 60 min; the midguts were collected, and then washed three times with RNase-free water. After RNA isolation and cDNA synthesis, the dsRNA absorbed in the midgut was assessed via probe RT-qPCR (AceQ qPCR Probe Master Mix, Vazyme) and quantified using the formula derived from the calibration experiment (Supplementary Fig. S1). A reverse primer (Table 2) used in the 250-bp dsRED synthesis was also used for the corresponding cDNA production. Table 3 shows the probe RT-qPCR primers. The experiment was conducted independently in five replicates.

Whole gut tissues were dissected from the last instar larvae of GA-1 T. castaneum, and tissue samples were placed in saline. dsRED (the dsRNA of red fluorescent protein) solutions were diluted using the EX-CELL® 405 Serum-Free Medium for Insect Cells (Sigma-Aldrich) according to a previously described method (Wang et al., 2018). Isolated gut tissues were rinsed three times with the medium before being extended on wax, and the solution was then pipetted (25 °C; relative humidity: 90–95%) until the entire midgut area was covered. Gut samples were cultured for 5, 30, and 60 min; the midguts were collected, and then washed three times with RNase-free water. After RNA isolation and cDNA synthesis, the dsRNA absorbed in the midgut was assessed via probe RT-qPCR (AceQ qPCR Probe Master Mix, Vazyme) and quantified using the formula derived from the calibration experiment (Supplementary Fig. S1). A reverse primer (Table 2) used in the 250-bp dsRED synthesis was also used for the corresponding cDNA production. Table 3 shows the probe RT-qPCR primers. The experiment was conducted independently in five replicates.

Significance analyses were performed using one-way analysis of variance and Tukey's test, and differences were considered significant at P-values <0.05. Values are reported as the mean ± standard error. Linear regression models were constructed using the curve estimation process. All statistical analyses were carried out using GraphPad Prism v6.0 software (La Jolla, CA, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Competitive inhibition occurred between the different dsRNA molecules
ce:section.ce:para: To determine whether competitive inhibition occurs between dsRNA molecules with the different sequences in T.castaneum, we treated the GA-1 individuals simultaneously with two dsRNAs: one 250 bp-dsRNA of the exogenous EYFP gene, as the competitor; and one 250 bp-dsRNA of the endogenous α-tubulin gene (TcTUB), as the reporter. As shown in Fig. 1 , when a 0.05-μg and 0.1-μg competitor dsRNA, ten- and five-fold lower than the dsTcTUB (0.5 μg) were injected, no competition at the mRNA level resulting in efficient TcTUB knockdown (85% and 81% depletion, respectively) was detected. In contrast, following injection of 0.5 μg, 2.5 μg, and 5 μg of the competitor dsRNA, which were one-, five-, and ten-fold higher than the reporter dsTcTUB, we detected competition that resulted in inefficient TcTUB knockdown at the mRNA level (69%, 64%, and 32% reduction, respectively). A similar result was observed in feeding individuals, and significant knock down occurred at the TcTUB mRNA level (35% and 32% reduction) when we fed competitor dsRNA at a ten- and five-fold lower amount (0.05 μg and 0.1 μg, respectively) than dsTcTUB (0.5 μg). However, feeding with the competitor dsRNA at the same (0.5 μg) or four-fold higher amount (2 μg) than dsTcTUB (0.5 μg) caused competitive inhibition at the mRNA level resulting in inefficient TcTUB knockdown (19% and 14% reduction, respectively). In brief, a decrease in knockdown efficacy was observed with an increasing amount of competitor dsRNA in the injected and feeding insects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Competitive inhibition could be decreased by conjugated chimeric dsRNAs
ce:section.ce:para: A chimeric dsRNA strategy conjugating multiple dsRNA fragments into a single dsRNA molecule could be used to decrease competition between different dsRNAs. A chimeric dsRNA molecule (dsC, 500 bp in length, Fig. 2A) was synthesized and treated with T. castaneum GA-1 individuals. As shown in Fig. 2B, when the chimeric dsRNA dsC (0.5 μg) was injected into T.castaneum, no significant competition at the mRNA level that resulted in efficient knockdown of TcTUB (83% depletion) was detected. In contrast, a strong significant competitive inhibition (65% depletion on TcTUB) was detected after an injection of a mixture of dsTcTUB (0.5 μg) and dsEYFP (0.5 μg). Similarly, no competition induced by the chimeric dsRNA at the mRNA level of TcTUB was determined in feeding individuals (47% depletion), compared with the inefficient knockdown of TcTUB (17% depletion) induced by co-injection of dsTcTUB and dsEYFP. Moreover, an EYFP transgenic T.castaneum strain Pu11 was used to test whether a chimeric dsRNA could knock down multiple target genes simultaneously. As shown in Fig. 3 , when the chimeric dsRNA dsC was injected into Pu11 individuals, approximately 79% and 81% depletion was detected at the mRNA level of TcTUB and EYFP, respectively. Meanwhile, significant knockdown was also found at the mRNA level of TcTUB (38% depletion) and EYFP (32% depletion) when the chimeric dsRNA was fed with the Pu11 beetles. In brief, our study demonstrated that the chimeric strategy could be an efficient method to knock down multiple target genes simultaneously.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Competitive inhibition could be decreased by CQD encapsulated dsRNAs
ce:section.ce:para: An NP CQD, which could significantly improve uptake efficacy of extracellular dsRNAs via an alternative uptake pathway, was used to deliver dsRNA in the T. castaneum Pu11 beetles. The result in Fig. 4 shows that when CQD-encapsulated dsEYFP and dsTcTUB were co-injected at the same concentration (0.5 μg/μL), no competitive inhibition at the mRNA level of TcTUB (80% reduction) was observed; however, inefficient TcTUB knockdown (69% deduction) was detected when the native dsEYFP (0.5 μg/μL) and dsTcTUB (0.5 μg/μL) were simultaneously injected. Meanwhile, no competition was found at the mRNA level of TcTUB (32% depletion) and EYFP (38% depletion) when the CQD-encapsulated dsEYFP and dsTcTUB were co-fed with the Pu11 beetles, compared with the inefficient knockdown of TcTUB (18% depletion) and EYFP (14% depletion) induced by co-fed of naked dsTcTUB and dsEYFP. This data briefly shows that competition between dsRNAs could be decreased by NP encapsulation, suggesting that competition might not occur following dsRNAs delivery into cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Accumulation of a series of treated dsRNA amounts in T. castaneum midgut
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, dsRED was incubated to avoid possible endogenous gene interference. The calibration lines for dsRNA quantification, developed using calibration experiments with serial dilutions of known dsRNA amounts, proved accurate (Supplementary Fig. S1). The linear ranges detected with the 250 bp dsRED fragment were between 0.01 and 10 ng.

In this study, dsRED was incubated to avoid possible endogenous gene interference. The calibration lines for dsRNA quantification, developed using calibration experiments with serial dilutions of known dsRNA amounts, proved accurate (Supplementary Fig. S1). The linear ranges detected with the 250 bp dsRED fragment were between 0.01 and 10 ng.

In vitro experiments were performed to evaluate the midgut accumulation of different amounts of treated dsRED. We incubated 1 μg-dissected midguts of T. castaneum with 0.5 μg, 1 μg, 2 μg, and 4 μg of dsRED (in 10 μl of EX-CELL® 405 Serum-Free Medium) for 5, 30, and 60 min. Accumulation was detected by evaluating dsRED content in the midgut samples after thoroughly rinsing in the nuclease-free water (Fig. 5 ). The initial dsRED concentration in the T. castaneum midgut at 5 min after incubation was approximately 11.6 pg/μg of fresh tissue weight, which may have resulted from quick adhesion to the midgut surface and limited cellular uptake associated with the short incubation time. Therefore, these initial concentrations were used as a basis to eliminate the effects of multiple extracellular factors. After 60 min, the T. castaneum midgut accumulation was assessed. The relative increase in the accumulation speed indexes [(concentration (60 min) – concentration (5 min))/ (60–5 min)] for 0.25 μg, 0.5 μg, 1 μg, 2 μg, and 4 μg of incubated dsRED amounts were calculated as 1, 1.09, 0.80, 0.78, and 0.53, respectively. These results indicated that the accumulation of dsRNAs of different treated amounts was in the following order: 0.25 μg ≈ 0.5 μg > 1 μg ≥ 2 μg > 4 μg.

A key discovery of this study is that competitions between dsRNA fragments could induce competitive inhibition in the RNAi-sensitive insect species T. castaneum. Besides, similar results have been reported in human cells, where competitions between dsRNAs occurred during intracellular processes (Koller et al., 2006). However, not all reported animal species show the same degree of sensitivity toward RNAi (Zhu and Palli, 2020). The oligoneopteran T. castaneum are fully sensitive (Scott et al., 2013), whereas mammalian cells are less sensitive to systemic RNAi (Jose, 2015). Thus, further studies are required to elucidate the precise mechanism of competitive inhibition induced by different dsRNAs in RNAi-sensitive insects.

Competition between dsRNAs could occur at three levels: dsRNA uptake, dsRNA processing to siRNA, and siRNA-mediated cleavage of target mRNA. Thus, factors associated with these processes may contribute to the aforementioned competitive phenomenon. A chimeric dsRNA strategy for conjugating multiple dsRNA fragments into a single dsRNA molecule was tested to test whether competition occurs during mRNA digestion. In brief, our data show that the chimeric strategy could be a useful way to decrease competition between dsRNAs in treated insects. DNA chimeras have been widely constructed in molecular cloning. In contrast, RNA chimera containing miRNA and its target mRNA fragment could be ligated and sequenced in experimental identifications of the human miRNA interactome. In entomological studies, the chimeric dsRNA strategy enhanced RNAi efficiency in Plutella xylostella, and precisely targeted several insect species (Sharath Chandra et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2021). In this study, although we confirmed that the chimeric strategy could knock down multiple target genes simultaneously, the possibility of siRNA-induced competition could be initially excluded by conducting chimeric dsRNA assays since the quality and quantity of the diced siRNAs between the treatments of the different dsRNAs and chimeric dsRNA were consistent. Furthermore, a powerful RNAi nanoparticle CQD, which could efficiently transport dsRNA molecules into insect cytoplasm via alternative uptake pathways, was used to deliver dsRNA in T. castaneum (Wang et al., 2019). Our data shows that competition between dsRNAs could be decreased by using a CQD-based delivery strategy. Thus, our study suggests that the competition between dsRNAs may not occur during intracellular processes, which means that dsRNA competition in T. castaneum mainly occurs at the level of dsRNA uptake; that is, strategies decreasing uptake competition between dsRNAs can be applied as possible solutions for simultaneous treatment of multiple dsRNAs in future applications of RNAi-based pest control. Two different dsRNA fragments have been tested in our dsRNA chimera and nanoparticle-mediated RNAi experiments; however, three or more different dsRNAs could be simultaneously applied in future applications. Our study provides a useful platform to avoid the potential competition among the different dsRNAs, but further experiments are still needed regarding different situations. The preferences of the two strategies would be different as well; for example, it would be convenient to construct the transgenic RNAi plants targeting several pests by expressing a chimeric dsRNA.

Our data also found that competitive inhibition was detected between different dsRNA fragments, suggesting that dsRNA potency affects the ability to enter insect cells. A study on human cell lines HeLa and U87MG has shown that a dsRNA with low RNAi efficacy cannot compete with a dsRNA inducing highly efficient RNAi, which indicates that some amount of an intracellular component, such as Ago-2, limits dsRNA activity (Koller et al., 2006; Vickers et al., 2007). However, instead of cellular components such as Dcr-2 and Ago-2, no evidence has shown that endocytic components can bind higher potent dsRNAs. Moreover, a liposome vector lipofectamine 2000 was used in their research to transfect dsRNA into HeLa and U87MG cells because of the low efficacy of dsRNA uptake by human cells. Thus, the competition at the level of cellular uptake was not determined in human cell lines. Therefore, we speculated that competition between dsRNAs occurs at the level of dsRNA uptake in the insects. We systemically determined the uptake efficacy of a concentration series of the same dsRNA fragments in T. castaneum by conducting an in vitro accumulation assay. This data showed that the accumulation of different dsRNA amounts was in the following order: 0.5 μg > 1 μg ≈ 2 μg > 4 μg. Meanwhile, the in vivo midgut accumulation assay is also consistent with the in vitro assay (Supplementary Fig. S2), suggesting that the dsRNA uptake capacities of T. castaneum are sensitive to the treated dsRNA amount. And another in vivo midgut accumulation assay was conducted to compare the uptake efficiency of naked dsRNA, CQD encapsulated dsRNA, and chimeric dsRNA, Supplementary Fig. S3 briefly demonstrated that the nanoparticle CQD and the chimeric strategy could significantly improve the cellular uptake of the reporter dsRNAs. Serial concentrations of the Eyfp gene dsRNA (250 bp in length) were used to treat the corresponding Pu11 T. castaneum and determine whether competition inhibition was detected between dsRNA molecules with the same sequence. PCR Results of the expression levels of the target genes (Supplementary Fig. S4) demonstrate a dramatic depletion (79% and 70%) in the dsEYFP-injected groups using approximately 0.5 μg and 2.5 μg treatment, respectively. A significant competition at the mRNA level that resulted in inefficient knockdown of EYFP (68% depletion) was detected after an injection of 5 μg dsEYFP. This implied that an inefficient knockdown was detected between the same dsRNA molecules in the red flour beetles. Thus, our data systemically demonstrated that dsRNA molecules would compete for the cellular uptake, causing inefficient knockdown on the target genes. And a previous study also found that sequential injection of the reporter dsRNA and the competitor dsRNA could decrease the amount of competition in insects, suggesting that competition might be occurring when the dsRNA is entering the insect's cell (Miller et al., 2012).

Our data also found that competitive inhibition was detected between different dsRNA fragments, suggesting that dsRNA potency affects the ability to enter insect cells. A study on human cell lines HeLa and U87MG has shown that a dsRNA with low RNAi efficacy cannot compete with a dsRNA inducing highly efficient RNAi, which indicates that some amount of an intracellular component, such as Ago-2, limits dsRNA activity (Koller et al., 2006; Vickers et al., 2007). However, instead of cellular components such as Dcr-2 and Ago-2, no evidence has shown that endocytic components can bind higher potent dsRNAs. Moreover, a liposome vector lipofectamine 2000 was used in their research to transfect dsRNA into HeLa and U87MG cells because of the low efficacy of dsRNA uptake by human cells. Thus, the competition at the level of cellular uptake was not determined in human cell lines. Therefore, we speculated that competition between dsRNAs occurs at the level of dsRNA uptake in the insects. We systemically determined the uptake efficacy of a concentration series of the same dsRNA fragments in T. castaneum by conducting an in vitro accumulation assay. This data showed that the accumulation of different dsRNA amounts was in the following order: 0.5 μg > 1 μg ≈ 2 μg > 4 μg. Meanwhile, the in vivo midgut accumulation assay is also consistent with the in vitro assay (Supplementary Fig. S2), suggesting that the dsRNA uptake capacities of T. castaneum are sensitive to the treated dsRNA amount. And another in vivo midgut accumulation assay was conducted to compare the uptake efficiency of naked dsRNA, CQD encapsulated dsRNA, and chimeric dsRNA, Supplementary Fig. S3 briefly demonstrated that the nanoparticle CQD and the chimeric strategy could significantly improve the cellular uptake of the reporter dsRNAs. Serial concentrations of the Eyfp gene dsRNA (250 bp in length) were used to treat the corresponding Pu11 T. castaneum and determine whether competition inhibition was detected between dsRNA molecules with the same sequence. PCR Results of the expression levels of the target genes (Supplementary Fig. S4) demonstrate a dramatic depletion (79% and 70%) in the dsEYFP-injected groups using approximately 0.5 μg and 2.5 μg treatment, respectively. A significant competition at the mRNA level that resulted in inefficient knockdown of EYFP (68% depletion) was detected after an injection of 5 μg dsEYFP. This implied that an inefficient knockdown was detected between the same dsRNA molecules in the red flour beetles. Thus, our data systemically demonstrated that dsRNA molecules would compete for the cellular uptake, causing inefficient knockdown on the target genes. And a previous study also found that sequential injection of the reporter dsRNA and the competitor dsRNA could decrease the amount of competition in insects, suggesting that competition might be occurring when the dsRNA is entering the insect's cell (Miller et al., 2012).

In C. elegans, a channel protein SID-1 binds long dsRNA for cellular uptake (Rocheleau, 2012). However, whether SID-1-like (SIL) proteins are involved in dsRNA uptake or transport in insects remains unclear (Tomoyasu et al., 2008). Instead of the SIL-mediated uptake mechanism of dsRNA, clathrin-dependent endocytosis has been implicated in dsRNA uptake in many insects, including D. melanogaster, T. castaneum, and Schistocerca gregaria (Saleh et al., 2006; Wynant et al., 2014; Xiao et al., 2015). Thus, the involvement of endocytic components, such as Clathrin heavy chain, Clathrin adaptor proteins, and dsRNA-specific receptors, might be sensitive to oversaturation following excess dsRNA treatment, thereby causing competitive inhibition of target genes. Our results indicated that the chimeric strategy which could relatively reduce molecular number of the treated dsRNA may decrease possibilities of the oversaturation, while the alternative dsRNA uptake mediated by CQD nanoparticles could also bypass the original uptake mechanism which causes the oversaturation. Moreover, the involvement and the functional feature of intracellular components, which strongly influence the RNAi effect of insects, vary among insect species. However, the precise mechanisms of both dsRNA uptake and intracellular dsRNA processing in insects remain unclear. Thus, molecular networks contributing to competitions between dsRNAs need further elucidation.

In conclusion, our study has shown that competitions between different dsRNA fragments could occur in the insect pest, influencing the effectiveness of RNAi response. Then, a chimeric dsRNA strategy and an NP CQD-mediated dsRNA delivery were confirmed as efficient methods to knock down multiple target genes simultaneously, suggesting that competitions between dsRNAs mainly occur at the level of cellular uptake in T. castaneum. Furthermore, our data showed that endocytic components involving cellular uptake might be oversaturated when excess dsRNAs were treated, thereby causing competitive inhibition of target genes. The study of these basic features of competitions between dsRNAs in T. castaneum will not only provide insight into the molecular mechanism of the competitive inhibition but will also aid in RNAi-based pest control.

The following are the supplementary data related to this article.
Supplementary Fig. S1
Calibration curve for dsRED quantification. Ct values were plotted against the log10 transformed quantity of dsRED (pg) in 10-μg samples. The data were described via the construction of linear regression models (Y = -2.930 + 24.38, R2 = 0.987). Values are reported as the mean ± SE (n ≥ 5).
Supplementary Fig. S1
Supplementary Fig. S2
Accumulation of 2-μg and 4-μg of injected dsRNAs in T. castaneum midgut. The two dsRED amounts were injected (in vivo) into T. castaneum Pu11 individuals over the experimental period. Different letters on the treatment group indicate significant differences at p < 0.05 (Tukey's test).
Supplementary Fig. S2
Supplementary Fig. S3
Accumulation of injected 250 bp-dsRED fragments in T. castaneum midgut. The dsRNAs were injected (in vivo) into T. castaneum Pu11 individuals over the experimental period. The chimeric dsRNA (dsC) comprising 250 bp of dsEYFP (dsE) and dsRED (dsR) were used. CQD: carbon quntaum dots encapsulated. Different letters on the treatment group indicate significant differences at p < 0.05 (Tukey's test).
Supplementary Fig. S3
Supplementary Fig. S4
Relative expressions of EYFP mRNA after injection with the indicated amount of dsE (dsEYFP) in Pu11 T. castaneum. dsRED was used as control. Each treatment involved at least five replicates, with three to five individuals included in a replicate. The relative expression was calculated based on the control. Different letters on the treatment group indicate significant differences at p < 0.05 (Tukey's test).
Supplementary Fig. S4
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.pestbp.2021.105009.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (2019YFC1605303), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32102296), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20210669), the National Grain Industry Foundation for Young Top-Notch Scholars of China (LQ2020102), the Youth Scholar Support Program of Nanjing University of Finance and Economics (NUFE), the Science-Technology Foundation for Outstanding Young Scientists of Henan Academy of Agricultural Sciences (2020YQ21), the Faculty Startup Program of NUFE, and the Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).
